title: Nimzo-Indian Defense: Classical
summary: Learn the Nimzo-Indian, where Black pins and threatens to double White's pawns in exchange for quick development.
description: The Nimzo-Indian is one of Black's most respected defenses to 1.d4. You'll learn why Black's move ...Bb4 pins the knight and forces White to make important decisions about the pawn structure.
line: 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6 3.Nc3 Bb4 4.e3 O-O 5.Nf3 d5 6.Bd3 c5
objectives: Understand the pin on the knight and why it's strategically dangerous.
Learn how to play with doubled c-pawns (or avoid them).
Master the strategic ideas in the Nimzo-Indian structure.
plans: White can accept doubled pawns for the bishop pair, or avoid it with e3 or Qc2.
Black seeks to keep the position closed while maintaining the pin.
The position often becomes a strategic battle where each side has clear plans.
commonMistakes: Do not ignore the pin. It only gets worse if you don't address it.
Avoid passive play. With the bishop pair, White should be active.
Do not underestimate Black's position. It's solid and has many plans.
completionTitle: Strategic battle with plans for both
completionSummary: The Nimzo-Indian creates deep strategic positions where understanding the ideas matters more than memorization.

steps.title: Control the center
steps.instruction: Start with d4, claiming central space.
steps.coaching: The queen's pawn opening is solid and leads to many strategic battles.
steps.hint: Push the queen pawn two squares.

steps.title: Black develops the knight
steps.instruction: The knight develops to f6, a standard move in Indian defenses.
steps.coaching: Black prepares to play ...e6 or ...b6, developing harmoniously.

steps.title: Play c4 to challenge the center
steps.instruction: Push c4, starting the Queen's Gambit structure.
steps.coaching: This is the standard move that leads to the Nimzo-Indian after Black's reply.
steps.hint: Push the c-pawn to challenge d4.

steps.title: Black prepares the Nimzo
steps.instruction: The pawn on e6 supports d5 and prepares ...Bb4.
steps.coaching: This is the setup for the Nimzo-Indian: Black will pin the knight on c3.

steps.title: Develop the knight
steps.instruction: Move Nc3, developing toward the center.
steps.coaching: Natural development. But now Black can play ...Bb4 with a pin.
steps.hint: Develop the queen's knight to c3.

steps.title: The Nimzo-Indian pin
steps.instruction: Black pins the knight, threatening to double the c-pawns.
steps.coaching: This is the defining move. The bishop pins the knight and controls the e4 square.
steps.hint: Move the bishop to b4, pinning the knight.

steps.title: Avoid doubling (or accept it)
steps.instruction: Play e3 to avoid the pin's immediate consequences.
steps.coaching: This move avoids doubling and prepares to develop the bishop. White accepts slightly slower development.
steps.hint: Push the e-pawn one square to avoid the pin.

steps.title: Black castles
steps.instruction: Black castles, getting the king safe.
steps.coaching: Black completes kingside development while White is still figuring out the structure.

steps.title: Develop the knight
steps.coaching: White continues development despite the pin. The position is strategically complex.

steps.title: Black claims central space
steps.instruction: Play d5, challenging White's central control.
steps.coaching: Black claims space in the center. The position is now balanced but complex.

steps.title: Develop the bishop
steps.instruction: Move Bd3, developing the bishop to an active square.
steps.coaching: The bishop develops to a good square, supporting the central position.

steps.title: Black breaks in the center
steps.instruction: Black challenges the center with c5, opening the position.
steps.coaching: This is a standard break in the Nimzo-Indian. Black seeks active piece play.
steps.hint: Push the c-pawn to challenge d4.
